perturbation.original_text: Sabco shall provide at its expense and maintain throughout --------- the term of this Agreement and any option period spectator liability insurance in an amount not less than $1 million single limit coverage with respect to any liability relating to the activities of Sabco in the performance of this Agreement. Sabco shall, within 90 days of the execution of this Agreement, supply Prolong with a copy of such policy of insurance or a certificate thereof, and such policies shall be cancelable only upon 10 days written notice to Prolong.
perturbation.changed_text: Sabco shall maintain spectator liability insurance with respect to any liability relating to the activities of Sabco in the performance of this Agreement. Sabco shall supply Prolong with a certificate of such policy of insurance.
perturbation.explanation: The omission of the minimum coverage amount ($1 million single limit) and the cancelation notice period weakens the insurance requirement. North Carolina law requires specific minimum liability coverage to protect the public.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina General Statutes § 99B-1 et seq. (Premises Liability)
perturbation.law_explanation: While NCGS § 99B-1 et seq. doesn't explicitly state a minimum insurance amount, it establishes premises liability standards. Removing the $1 million minimum coverage could be seen as insufficient to cover potential liabilities, creating a legal risk of not meeting the duty of care required by premises liability laws, particularly in events with spectators. This absence could leave Prolong vulnerable if the actual insurance is inadequate. Also, the removal of the cancelation notification does not allow for Prolong to be informed if insurance coverage lapses, which introduces risk.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the minimum coverage amount ($1 million) and the cancellation notice period weakens the insurance requirement, creating a potential contradiction. The Law Explanation specifies this could create legal risk and leave Prolong vulnerable. The original contract text stipulates a minimum coverage amount of $1 million and a 10-day written notice for cancellation, which are specific protections. The changed text removes these protections, which weakens Prolong's protection and contradicts industry standards of liability insurance.

perturbation.original_text: 17. Governing Law, Jurisdiction and Venue. This Agreement shall be ------------------------------------- governed by and construed in accordance with the substantive laws of the State of North Carolina. The parties hereto hereby submit to jurisdiction and venue in any state or federal court located in North Carolina as well as any other jurisdiction having venue and competent jurisdiction of any law suit arising out of or relating to this Agreement; provided, however, if any proceedings are instituted in a jurisdiction other than North Carolina, any party may remove such proceeding to any State or Federal Court in North Carolina.
perturbation.changed_text: 17. Governing Law, Jurisdiction and Venue. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of North Carolina. The parties hereto hereby submit to jurisdiction and venue in any court.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'substantive' and deleting the forum selection clause that strongly favors North Carolina (giving either party the right to remove proceedings to NC), it weakens the enforceability and predictability of dispute resolution. This creates potential conflict with laws ensuring fair venue and jurisdiction, especially if disputes arise outside of North Carolina where other jurisdictions might have a stronger claim.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1404 allows a district court to transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought for the convenience of parties and witnesses and in the interest of justice. The modified clause eliminates the strong North Carolina forum selection, opening the door for litigation in potentially inconvenient or biased venues and contradicting principles of fairness and convenience in venue selection.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., §§ 119 , 163 (Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 58, 36 Stat. 1103 ; Sept. 8, 1916, ch. 475, § 5, 39 Stat. 851 ). Section co
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a forum selection clause that favors North Carolina, which allowed any party to remove proceedings to a North Carolina court. This creates a contradiction because, without the clause, litigation can occur in any venue, potentially inconvenient or biased, which may contradict the principles of fairness. 28 U.S.C. § 1404 states that a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought 'for the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice'. By removing the strong forum selection, the modified text opens the door for litigation in places not convenient, thus undermining the principles of fairness that the law seeks to protect.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Confidentiality. During and after the term of this Agreement, each --------------- party hereto, its employees and agents agree not to disclose any business results, trade practices or other business information of the other party, its employees or agents, which they may learn as a result of the performance of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Confidentiality. During the term of this Agreement, each party hereto, its employees and agents agree not to disclose any business results, trade practices or other business information of the other party, its employees or agents, which they may learn as a result of the performance of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'and after the term of this Agreement', the confidentiality obligation ceases at the end of the contract. This contradicts legal principles protecting trade secrets and confidential information, which often extend beyond the contract term under common law and statutory protections.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted in most states, including North Carolina)
perturbation.law_explanation: North Carolina's version of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (N.C. Gen. Stat. § 66-152) provides legal remedies for the misappropriation of trade secrets. By limiting the confidentiality clause to only the term of the agreement, the modified contract fails to adequately protect trade secrets after the agreement ends, potentially conflicting with the protections offered by the UTSA. Even without a specific non-disclosure agreement, trade secrets are protected, but the contract weakens the explicit protection after the term expires.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ G.S. 66-152 Article 24. Trade Secrets Protection Act. § 66-152. Definitions. As used in this Article, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Misappropriation" means acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied authority or consent, unless such trade secret was arrived at by independent development, reverse engineering, or was obtained from another person with a right to disclose the trade secret. (2) "Person" means an individual, corporation, government, governmental subdivision or agency, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, or any other legal or commercial entity. (3) "Trade secret" means business or technical information, including but not limited to a formula, pattern, program, device, compilation of information, method, technique, or process that: a. Derives independent actual or potential commercial value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable through independent development or reverse engineering by persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and b. Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. The existence of a trade secret shall not be negated merely because the information comprising the trade secret has also been developed, used, or owned independently by more than one person, or licensed to other persons. (1981, c. 890, s. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the phrase 'and after the term of this Agreement' from the confidentiality clause. This significantly weakens the protection of trade secrets after the agreement ends. The Law Explanation states this can conflict with the North Carolina Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which protects trade secrets even without a specific non-disclosure agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as the 'disclosure, or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied authority or consent'. By limiting confidentiality to the term of the agreement, the modified contract potentially allows for misappropriation after the agreement ends, contradicting the UTSA's broader protection.
